Task: How to remove type suggestions in VScode?

code auto filling

I'm learning rust and I'm using VScode to do so, and when I type an assigning statement, it keeps recommending me to assign it a type such as i32, int, etc. I'm following the online rust guidebook, so I know this isn't necessary, and it's kinda annoying because it keeps getting in the way. I was wondering if anyone knows how to turn it off. I've looked elsewhere online and haven't been able to find anything. All the resources I've found online have been how to disable autofilling during typing but I'm getting autofilling even after the line is done.

Thx!!

prompt:
Task: What does an "implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough" error mean?

Let's say we have a trait like the following, which is a distillation of how view types work in protobuf::Message:

trait Message {
    type View<'a>;
}

We are implementing an RPC system, so we define a trait for functions that know how to deal with an incoming message. (In reality we need this to make more requirements on the result type, like that it be Future + Send).

trait HandlerFn<Request> {}

impl<Request, F, R> HandlerFn<Request> for F
where
    Request: Message,
    F: Fn(Request::View<'_>) -> R,
{
}

We try to check whether the simplest possible handler implements this trait:

fn check_handler_fn<Request>(_: impl HandlerFn<Request>) {}

fn foo<Request: Message>() {
    check_handler_fn::<Request>(|_| -> () { todo!() });
}

But this fails to build, with the following not-very-helpful error message:

error: implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough
  --> src/lib.rs:17:5
   |
17 |     check_handler_fn::<Request>(|_| -> () { todo!() });
   |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ implementation of `FnOnce` is not general enough
   |
   = note: closure with signature `fn(<Request as Message>::View<'0>)` must implement `FnOnce<(<Request as Message>::View<'_>,)>`, for some specific lifetime `'0`...
   = note: ...but it actually implements `FnOnce<(<Request as Message>::View<'1>,)>`, for some specific lifetime `'1`

(Playground)

What does this error mean? How can I repair the problem, teaching the compiler that the closure doesn't care about a particular lifetime?

prompt:
Task: What idioms exist to make extensible options structs in Rust?

Let's say I maintain an API with a bunch of options, and that set of options may grow over time. I'd like to give a convenient way for users to override some but not all options, but without restricting me from adding new options in the future with suitable defaults. It should be convenient to set options, but ideally not require a ton of boilerplate on my part. What idioms exist for doing this in Rust?

As someone coming from C++ where I would just make this a normal struct with suitable defaults, I'm tempted to reach for struct update syntax for the user-facing aspect of conveniently overriding only some default options:

MyType::new(MyTypeOptions{
    a: 1,
    c: 2,
    ..Default::default()
})

If all users wrote it this way I'd be fine, and could add new fields. But I can't require them to do this, as far as I can see. So then I'm tempted to reach for non_exhaustive, which sounds like it solves exactly this problem. Except, annoyingly, it prevents the syntax above. This article discusses the problem, and at the bottom hints at an answer to my question, but is rather cryptic and doesn't actually spell out the idiom.

Task: rust Command fails in build.rs (No such file or directory)

I'm trying to build a frontend project before I build my rust project.
Here is the code in my build.rs file.

if frontend_path.exists() {
    println!("Frontend path: {}", frontend_path.to_str().unwrap());
    println!("frontend_path exists");
}
Command::new("bun run build")
    .current_dir(frontend_path)
    .status()
    .expect("Process failed to execute");

However I'm getting the following error.

  --- stderr

  thread 'main' (16546) panicked at build.rs:24:10:
  Process failed to execute: Os { code: 2, kind: NotFound, message: "No such file or directory" }
  note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace

It says No such file or directory even though it prints frontend_path exists

  --- stdout
  Frontend path: /it/printed/my/project/absolute/path
  frontend_path exists

Task: Implement an arbitrary order to my enum fields?

Just started learning Rust and I am trying to implement an order to each field of my enum. So for example if I have the following enum:

enum Fruit {
    Apple,
    Banana,
    Kiwi,
}

How can I assign an order to each of my fruits in such a way that I can use the "sort_by()" method on these fields after putting them in a vector for example? I hope I am clear I tried to implement an Ord trait for my enum but I am not sure to be on the right path and I can't seem to make it work with pattern matching.

prompt:
Task: I'm building a launcher with Rust and using Slint for the UI right now, does anyone have tips for using it well or recommend better alternatives?

I'm making a Minecraft launcher as a personal project right now, and I have the basic backend complete so I decided to start on the frontend. I obviously didn't want to use Electron because the whole point of this launcher is for it to be lightweight and fast, but look good as well. I considered Tauri and Svelte, but it's still slower than native Rust. I finally settled on Slint, but I'm having quite a few issues with figuring out how to make the UI look good when resized to be full-screen, or in slightly unconventional manners.

I would like to keep the window resizable, but even full-screening it at 2K just looks ugly, everything is far too spaced-out and I can't figure out how to make font scale up, or if there's a better way to do that. I'm also using Vertical/HorizontalLayouts, as well as vertical/horizontal-stretch, but it still just looks bad.

I've attached images of what the app currently looks like. I'm mainly looking for information on how to make the UI scale properly, or recommendations on other frameworks I could switch to (I don't mind rewriting the UI as long as I know it'll be easier or better.)

UI at 1080p

UI at almost 4K

UI at something pretty small

prompt:
Task: Docker missing context

when trying to run docker compose, docker misses context and actually copying other files

compose:

 bot:
    build:
      context: .
      target: bot
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - db
      - api

dockerfile:

FROM rust:1.93-bookworm as builder
WORKDIR /app

COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
COPY api bot cron entity ./

RUN cargo build --release 


FROM debian:bookworm-slim as base
WORKDIR /app


FROM base as bot 
COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/bot /usr/local/bin/bot
CMD ["bot"]

project structure:

dockerignore:

migration/
target/
.env

so when i do docker compose up or docker compose build --no-cache i get error

ERROR [bot bot 1/1] COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/bot /usr/local/bin/bot

verifying sha of Cargo.tomls in result i see that it actualy was copied from /entity folder, and building only entity project

doing -p for cargo build is not helping

if i explicitly specify directory, then it works fine, but i guess thats not a good solution
